summary: This chapter follows the protagonist investigating their mother's disappearance while uncovering family secrets. After a conversation with Dave about their fathers both working at the mysterious 'Operant' company, the narrator searches through their mother's dresser drawers. They discover a document showing their mother receives $10,000 monthly from something called the Triangle Trust. When they can't reach their mother and try to track her car through the Mercedes app, they find the account has been changed to an unknown email address, 'skinner666@gmail.com', suggesting something sinister is happening.
key_points: Both Dave's father and someone named Tom worked at 'Operant' and both apparently 'went crazy' - suggesting the company may be connected to psychological damage
The protagonist's family went from wealthy (yacht in Monaco, Cobble Hill mansion) to middle-class (Sheepshead Bay) after their father left, but money kept coming through the Triangle Trust
The narrator admits true crime podcasts have 'done bad things' for their personality and skewed their view of people
The mother's car tracking account has been changed to an unknown email 'skinner666@gmail.com' - a clear sign of foul play or deception
The parents never officially divorced - the father 'just left' but continued financial support
money_quotes: Resolving to be less of a pussy, I answered it.
It was always strange to me that that generation would just drop by. Like she didn't text her first?
I know that the people on there are out of the normal distribution of people, but those podcasts are one of my only exposures to the outside world.
I'd always just assumed my Dad was like, a Wall Street guy. Boring. Get money, fuck bitches.
My mom loved the luxury lifestyle, and would put up with a lot of my Dad's eccentricities to keep it.
As far as I knew, they never got a divorce. He just left.
A jolt of anxiety coarsed through my body. I'd never seen that e-mail address in my life.
key_insight: The mundane act of searching through a parent's belongings reveals how little we know about the people closest to us and the secrets that structure our lives.
